hub_subtitle: Conversação para Feiras Internacionais de Turismo & Hotelaria

lesson.menu_title: Sharing Your Vision and Inspiring the Room
lesson.menu_desc: Inspire an audience using inversion for emphasis -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 20 -- Sharing Your Vision
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Andrea Aggio | Aula 20 -- Sharing Your Vision
phases: The Room You Want to Move

listenings.text: I want to close by telling you where we're going. But first, let me tell you where we were. Two years ago, this industry was on its knees. Bookings vanished, partners pulled out, and there were mornings I wasn't sure we'd make it. I won't pretend that was easy, because resilience doesn't mean anything if the struggle wasn't real. And then came the turning point. We made one bold decision to stop competing on price and start leading on trust. Never before have we grown the way we've grown since. Not only did we recover, but we also came out with better partners and a clearer purpose than we've ever had. So here is the vision. Within three years, I want us to be the most trusted operator in the region, the name people reach for first. That is the legacy I want us to build together. And here is the thing about a moment like this. Rarely do you get a chance this good. So let's not applaud it. Let's go and make it inevitable.

Let's talk about inversion for emphasis, the grammar of great speeches. Normally, English word order is subject first, then verb. We have never seen demand like this. That's fine, but it's flat. Now watch what happens when you take the negative phrase and move it to the front. Never before have we seen demand like this. Did you feel that? When a negative or limiting phrase goes first, the word order flips. The auxiliary jumps in front of the subject, exactly like a question. Have we, not we have. Did we, not we did. It works with never, rarely, seldom, little, and with not only, followed by but also. It works with only, as in only by working together did we get here. One warning. This is a spice, not a sauce. Use it once or twice, at the peak moments where you want the room to lean in. Invert every sentence and it stops sounding powerful and starts sounding strange. Place it well, and you can make a simple claim feel like history.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a
a clear, ambitious picture of the future you want to build
b
the main speech that sets the theme of an event
c
to fill people with the desire and confidence to act
d
the lasting impact you leave behind
e
a moment of decisive change in a story or journey
f
to bring people together and energize them around a cause
g
the ability to recover and keep going after difficulty
h
the force of steady, ongoing progress
i
to actively support and promote a cause or idea
j
brave, confident, and willing to take risks
k
to picture something clearly as a future possibility
l
a strong hope or ambition to achieve something

reading.rtitle: The Room You Want to Move
paras: Anyone can read numbers off a slide. What separates a manager from a leader is the ability to stand in front of a room and make people believe in a future they cannot yet see. That is what a vision is: not a forecast, but a picture -- bold, clear, and specific enough that the people who hear it can imagine their own place inside it. When you share a vision well, you are not reporting the past; you are recruiting people into what comes next. You give them a reason to bring their best, and you rally them around something bigger than this quarter's target.

The best vision speeches are built on a simple emotional arc: where we were, the turning point, and where we are going. They name the difficulty honestly -- the downturn, the doubt, the hard year -- because resilience only inspires when the struggle was real. Then they mark the moment things changed, and they let the momentum carry the room forward. Notice how the great ones speak at the peak moments. They don't say 'we have never seen demand like this.' They say 'Never before have we seen demand like this.' The words are almost the same; the feeling is not. Fronting the emphasis and inverting the order makes the claim land like a drumbeat.

You do not need to be a natural orator to do this. You need a clear aspiration, the courage to say it out loud, and a few well-placed moments of emphasis. Champion the thing you actually believe in. Envision it concretely, in a sentence someone could repeat over coffee. And remember that the point of inspiring a room is not applause -- it is what people do on Monday morning. The leaders whose legacy outlasts them are simply the ones who gave others a future worth working toward, and made them feel, for a moment, that reaching it was inevitable.
reading.source: Adapted from a leadership communication guide

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: A good leader inspires a room only by reporting last quarter's numbers accurately.
Sharing a vision means painting a bold, clear picture of the future, naming the real struggle, and using well-placed emphasis to make people believe and act.
The main goal of a vision speech is to win applause in the room.

items: The text says a vision is the same thing as a forecast.
f
It says a vision is not a forecast but a picture -- bold, clear, and specific enough for people to imagine their place in it.
According to the text, naming the real difficulty makes resilience more inspiring.
t
The second paragraph says resilience only inspires when the struggle was real, so the best speeches name the difficulty honestly.
The text says 'Never before have we seen demand like this' feels different from the plain version.
t
It says the words are almost the same but the feeling is not -- fronting the emphasis and inverting makes the claim land like a drumbeat.
The text says the point of inspiring a room is the applause you get.
f
It says the point is not applause but what people do on Monday morning.

: the company stopped following the market and started leading it. She names the hard years honestly -- not only did they survive the downturn, but they also built
and better partners. She makes the aspiration concrete: she
the company as the region's most trusted operator in three years. For emphasis at the peak, she inverts: '
before have we had a chance like this.' Her goal is a
the whole team can be proud to build.

gapfill.text: Inversion for emphasis: front a negative/limiting phrase, then put the auxiliary before the subject -- 'Never before HAVE WE seen', 'Not only DID WE recover, but we also...', 'Only by being bold DID WE get here.' Same words, bigger impact. Use it at peak moments only.

bank.label: Useful language for sharing a vision
items: Let me tell you where we're going -- but first, where we were.
Never before have we had a chance like this one.
Not only did we survive, but we also came out stronger.
The turning point came when we chose to be bold.
I envision us as the region's most trusted operator.
Only by moving together did we get this far.
That is the legacy I want us to build.
Rarely do you get a moment this good -- let's not waste it.

lf.title: Read each line and complete the inversion
items: A
Never before have we
reached so many travelers.
strong
B
Not only did we survive
, but we also came out stronger.
strong
C
Rarely do you see
a team this determined.
soft
D
Only by being bold did we
build this momentum.
soft

lf.text: After a fronted negative or limiting phrase, invert: put the auxiliary (have, did, do) BEFORE the subject -- 'have we', 'did we', 'do you' -- just like a question. That inversion is what gives the line its punch.

items.situation: Open your vision speech. Set the arc.
tips: Where we were -> turning point -> where we go.
'Let me tell you where we were.'

items.situation: Deliver a peak claim with 'Never before'.
Add a bigger point with 'Not only'.
tips: did we + base verb, then but also.

items.situation: Explain how you got here with 'Only by'.
Close by rallying the room to the legacy.

items: Scenario 1
Open with where you were and the turning point -- name the struggle honestly, then mark the moment it changed.
Scenario 2
Hit a peak moment with inversion: 'Never before have we...' or 'Not only did we..., but we also...'.
Scenario 3
Make the aspiration concrete: 'I envision us as... within [time]', so people can picture it.
Scenario 4
Close by rallying the room around the legacy -- what you want them to do, not just feel.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = vision, 2 = momentum, 3 = envisions, 4 = Never, 5 = legacy.
Never + invert: 'Never before have we had a chance like this.'
Not only + invert + but also: 'Not only did we survive, but we also grew.'
Only by + invert main clause: 'Only by being bold did we get here.'
answerkey.note: Inversion: after a fronted negative/limiting phrase (never, rarely, not only, only by/when), the auxiliary comes before the subject -- have we, did we, do you.
